commands.repair_reason: Keep this PR open for contributor-owned real behavior proof and normal maintainer review; there is no narrow code repair for ClawSweeper to apply right now.; Cleared: No new security or supply-chain regression was found in the current two-file diff; the production change narrows an existing attribute-XSS sink. (sha=dc360ad6dac3527875437f85eaff2d7ff6126520)
commands.review_summary:
The PR escapes `entry.id` at the HTML export `id` and `data-entry-id` attribute sinks and adds linkedom regression coverage for XSS prevention and copy-link round-trip.

Reproducibility: yes. by source inspection: current main inserts raw `entry.id` into the exported HTML `id` and `data-entry-id` attributes. I did not execute a real browser exploit path in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: Keep this PR open for contributor-owned real behavior proof and normal maintainer review; there is no narrow code repair for ClawSweeper to apply right now.

target.title: fix(security): escape entry.id in HTML export to prevent attribute XSS
target.body:
## Problem

`renderEntry` and `renderCopyLinkButton` in `src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.js` interpolate `entry.id` directly into `id="..."` and `data-entry-id="..."` HTML attributes without escaping. A crafted entry ID like `"><script>alert(1)</script>` causes attribute breakout and XSS in exported HTML files.

## Fix

Route `entry.id` through `escapeHtmlAttr()` at the two HTML insertion boundaries:

1. **`renderEntry`** (line 1323): `entry-${escapeHtmlAttr(entry.id)}` for the element `id` attribute
2. **`renderCopyLinkButton`** (line 1312): `escapeHtmlAttr(entryId)` for the `data-entry-id` attribute

The raw `entry.id` is passed to `renderCopyLinkButton` as a parameter (not pre-escaped), so escaping happens exactly once inside the function. Browsers decode HTML entities in `dataset` reads, so `dataset.entryId` returns the original raw ID for `buildShareUrl()`.

Production diff is +2 lines changed across 1 file (template.js). No CHANGELOG changes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Crafted `entry.id` values containing `"`, `<`, `>` characters could break out of HTML attributes in exported conversation files, enabling XSS via attribute injection. The fix applies `escapeHtmlAttr()` at both HTML insertion points in the client-side template.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit dc360ad) at `/tmp/fix-83104`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-83104
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

# 2. Verify escapeHtmlAttr is applied at both injection points in compiled template
grep -n "escapeHtmlAttr" dist/export-html/template.js

# 3. Verify no raw script injection in generated export HTML
node -e "
const fs = require('fs');
const templateHtml = fs.readFileSync('dist/export-html/template.html', 'utf8');
const session = {
  title: 'XSS Proof',
  entries: [{
    id: '\"><script>alert(1)</script><div data-x=\"',
    type: 'message', role: 'user', timestamp: Date.now(),
    content: [{ type: 'text', text: 'test' }]
  }]
};
const b64 = Buffer.from(JSON.stringify(session)).toString('base64');
const html = templateHtml.replace('{{SESSION_DATA_BASE64}}', b64);
const scriptCount = (html.match(/<script>alert/g) || []).length;
console.log('Raw <script>alert() in HTML:', scriptCount === 0 ? '0 (PASS)' : scriptCount + ' (FAIL)');
console.log('Session data stored as base64 (not raw HTML):', html.includes(b64) ? 'YES (PASS)' : 'NO (FAIL)');
"

# 4. Run security test suite
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Compiled production template verification

Both injection points use `escapeHtmlAttr()` in the compiled `dist/export-html/template.js`:

```console
$ grep -n "escapeHtmlAttr" dist/export-html/template.js
668:  function escapeHtmlAttr(text) {
1312:    return `<button class="copy-link-btn" data-entry-id="${escapeHtmlAttr(entryId)}" title="Copy link to this message">
1323:    const entryId = `entry-${escapeHtmlAttr(entry.id)}`;
```

Line 1312 (`data-entry-id`) and line 1323 (element `id`) both route through `escapeHtmlAttr()`. Before this fix, both interpolated raw `entry.id` directly into HTML attributes.

The `escapeHtmlAttr` function (line 668) entity-encodes `&`, `<`, `>`, `"`, and `'` characters, preventing attribute breakout regardless of the entry ID content.

### Export HTML generation with XSS payload

Generated an HTML export containing a crafted entry ID with an XSS script injection payload:

```console
$ node -e "<export generation script>"
Raw <script>alert() in HTML: 0 (PASS)
Session data stored as base64 (not raw HTML): YES (PASS)
```

The session data (including the malicious entry ID) is stored as a base64-encoded JSON blob in the HTML, not as raw markup. When the client-side template.js decodes and renders entries, `escapeHtmlAttr()` entity-encodes the ID before inserting it into attributes.

### Vitest security test suite (10 tests, supplemental)

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts
 ✓ |auto-reply| ../../src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts (10 tests) 234ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

The security tests use linkedom to parse rendered HTML and verify:
- No `<script>` tags injected from entry IDs
- `data-entry-id` attributes contain entity-encoded values
- Element `id` attributes contain entity-encoded values
- `buildShareUrl()` copy-link round-trips correctly after encoding

Observed result after fix: The compiled production template applies `escapeHtmlAttr()` at both injection points. Session data is stored as base64-encoded JSON, and the rendering function entity-encodes entry IDs before attribute insertion. No raw `<script>` tags appear in generated HTML with malicious entry IDs. All 10 security tests pass.

What was not tested: Opening a generated export HTML file in a real browser and verifying the console shows no XSS execution. The linkedom-based security tests in the test suite verify DOM-level attribute escaping, which is the structural guarantee that prevents XSS.
target.author: sebtardif
